infobox.value: [/ˈmænzfiːld/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") is a [market town](/wiki/Market_town "Market town") and the administrative centre of the [Mansfield District](/wiki/Mansfield_District "Mansfield District") in [Nottinghamshire](/wiki/Nottinghamshire "Nottinghamshire"), England. It is the largest town in the wider [Mansfield Urban Area](/wiki/Mansfield_Urban_Area "Mansfield Urban Area")[[3]](#cite_note-EX270-3) and the second largest settlement in Nottinghamshire (following the city of [Nottingham](/wiki/Nottingham "Nottingham")). [Henry III](/wiki/Henry_III_of_England "Henry III of England") granted Mansfield the [Royal Charter](/wiki/Royal_charter "Royal charter") of a market town in 1227. The town lies in the [Maun Valley](/wiki/River_Maun "River Maun"), 12 miles (19 km) north of Nottingham. The district had a population of 110,500 at the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_United_Kingdom_census "2021 United Kingdom census").[[4]](#cite_note-4) Mansfield is the one local authority in Nottinghamshire with a publicly [elected mayor](/wiki/Directly_elected_mayors_in_England "Directly elected mayors in England"), the [Mayor of Mansfield](/wiki/Mayor_of_Mansfield "Mayor of Mansfield"). Mansfield in ancient times became the pre-eminent in importance amongst the towns of [Sherwood Forest](/wiki/Sherwood_Forest "Sherwood Forest").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

**Mansfield** [/ˈmænzfiːld/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") is a [market town](/wiki/Market_town "Market town") and the administrative centre of the [Mansfield District](/wiki/Mansfield_District "Mansfield District") in [Nottinghamshire](/wiki/Nottinghamshire "Nottinghamshire"), England. It is the largest town in the wider [Mansfield Urban Area](/wiki/Mansfield_Urban_Area "Mansfield Urban Area")[[3]](#cite_note-EX270-3) and the second largest settlement in Nottinghamshire (following the city of [Nottingham](/wiki/Nottingham "Nottingham")). [Henry III](/wiki/Henry_III_of_England "Henry III of England") granted Mansfield the [Royal Charter](/wiki/Royal_charter "Royal charter") of a market town in 1227. The town lies in the [Maun Valley](/wiki/River_Maun "River Maun"), 12 miles (19 km) north of Nottingham. The district had a population of 110,500 at the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_United_Kingdom_census "2021 United Kingdom census").[[4]](#cite_note-4) Mansfield is the one local authority in Nottinghamshire with a publicly [elected mayor](/wiki/Directly_elected_mayors_in_England "Directly elected mayors in England"), the [Mayor of Mansfield](/wiki/Mayor_of_Mansfield "Mayor of Mansfield"). Mansfield in ancient times became the pre-eminent in importance amongst the towns of [Sherwood Forest](/wiki/Sherwood_Forest "Sherwood Forest").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Toponymy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Toponymy")]

According to historian William Horner Groves (1894) there is dispute to the origins of the name. Three conjectures have been considered: the name may have been given to the noble family of Mansfield who came over with [William the Conqueror](/wiki/William_the_Conqueror "William the Conqueror"), other sources suggest that the name came from Manson, an [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon "Anglo-Saxon") word for traffic and a field meaning a place of trade, while others claim the town is named after the [River Maun](/wiki/River_Maun "River Maun") which runs through Mansfield, the town being built around the river.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### Roman to Medieval period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Roman to Medieval period")]

Settlement dates to [Roman Britain](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain") times between AD 43 to AD 410. [Hayman Rooke](/wiki/Hayman_Rooke "Hayman Rooke") in 1787 discovered two [Roman villas](/wiki/Roman_villa "Roman villa") between [Mansfield Woodhouse](/wiki/Mansfield_Woodhouse "Mansfield Woodhouse") and [Pleasley](/wiki/Pleasley "Pleasley"); a cache of [denarii](/wiki/Denarii "Denarii") (300-400 Roman Silver Coins were found near King's Mill in 1849).[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) A Roman tessellated pavement was found in one of the villas near [Mansfield Woodhouse](/wiki/Mansfield_Woodhouse "Mansfield Woodhouse").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

In AD 868 the [Danes](/wiki/Danes_(tribe) "Danes (tribe)") came into the county and by AD 877 they had complete control over the county. Their occupation left names on the town such as Skerry Hill, Ratcliffe Gate and Carr Bank.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Roman_Mosaic_Pavement_Mansfield_Woodhouse.jpg/250px-Roman_Mosaic_Pavement_Mansfield_Woodhouse.jpg)](/wiki/File:Roman_Mosaic_Pavement_Mansfield_Woodhouse.jpg)

The Roman tessellated pavement found in Mansfield Woodhouse

The Royal Manor of Mansfield was held by the King. In 1042, [Edward the Confessor](/wiki/Edward_the_Confessor "Edward the Confessor") possessed a manor in Mansfield. During the [Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest") in 1066, [William the Conqueror](/wiki/William_the_Conqueror "William the Conqueror") made [Sherwood Forest](/wiki/Sherwood_Forest "Sherwood Forest") a [Royal Forest](/wiki/Royal_Forest "Royal Forest") for hunting.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Mansfield was recorded as being *Mammesfeld* in the Wapentake of Broxtowe and the land of William the Conqueror in the [Domesday Book](/wiki/Domesday_Book "Domesday Book") of 1086. William owned *two carucates, five sochmans, and thirty-five villains; twenty borders, with nineteen carucates and a half in demesne, a mill, piscary, twenty-four acres of meadow and pasture'* in Mansfield.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

In the time of [Henry II of England](/wiki/Henry_II_of_England "Henry II of England"), the king visited what is now known as Kings Mill, staying at the home of Sir John Cockle for a night having been hunting in [Sherwood Forest](/wiki/Sherwood_Forest "Sherwood Forest"). Sir John Cockle was later known as the Miller of Mansfield.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) In 1199 the Manor was owned by [King John](/wiki/John,_King_of_England "John, King of England"). King John used to visit Mansfield frequently between 1200 and 1216, that he built a residence here. Later, [Edward I](/wiki/Edward_I "Edward I") held a Royal Council in the town. The Manor, then owned by [Henry III](/wiki/Henry_III_of_England "Henry III of England"), subsequently passed to [Henry de Hastings](/wiki/Henry_de_Hastings "Henry de Hastings"). In 1329 [Queen Isabella](/wiki/Isabella_of_France "Isabella of France"), mother of [Edward III](/wiki/Edward_III "Edward III"), was the Lady of the Manor of Mansfield.[[18]](#cite_note-William_Horner_Groves_1894-18)

Market-petition documents of 1227 spelt Mansfield *Maunnesfeld*. [Richard II](/wiki/Richard_II "Richard II") signed a warrant in November 1377 to grant tenants the right to hold a four-day fair each year; the spelling had changed to *Mannesfeld*.[[19]](#cite_note-Guide-19) Mansfield, Skegby and Sutton in Ashfield were the land of the king in 1086 as stated in the [Domesday Book](/wiki/Domesday_Book "Domesday Book").[[20]](#cite_note-Lady_Antonia_Fraser_1992-20) There are remains of the 12th-century [King John's Palace](/wiki/King_John%27s_Palace "King John's Palace") in [Kings Clipstone](/wiki/Kings_Clipstone "Kings Clipstone"), between Mansfield and [Edwinstowe](/wiki/Edwinstowe "Edwinstowe"), and it was an area of retreat for royal families and dignitaries through to the 15th century. It was here that [William the Lion of Scotland](/wiki/William_the_Lion "William the Lion") met [Richard I of England](/wiki/Richard_I_of_England "Richard I of England") (Richard the Lionheart) to congratulate him on his return from the crusades. It was also where [Queen Eleanor](/wiki/Eleanor_of_Castile "Eleanor of Castile") the first wife of [Edward I](/wiki/Edward_I_of_England "Edward I of England") was taken ill and moved to [Harby](/wiki/Harby,_Nottinghamshire "Harby, Nottinghamshire").[[21]](#cite_note-21) King John and Edward I are reputed to have had impromptu parliaments at the [Parliament Oak](/wiki/Parliament_Oak "Parliament Oak") near [Market Warsop](/wiki/Market_Warsop "Market Warsop").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

[St Peter and St Paul's Church](/wiki/St_Peter_and_St_Paul%27s_Church,_Mansfield "St Peter and St Paul's Church, Mansfield") is mentioned in the 1086 Doomsday Book and in 1092 it was passed by [William II](/wiki/William_II_of_England "William II of England") to [Robert Bloet](/wiki/Robert_Bloet "Robert Bloet") the bishop of [Lincoln](/wiki/Lincoln,_England "Lincoln, England") and [Lord Chancellor of England](/wiki/Lord_Chancellor_of_England "Lord Chancellor of England").[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[20]](#cite_note-Lady_Antonia_Fraser_1992-20)[[24]](#cite_note-auto-24)

Access to the town was by road from the city of [Nottingham](/wiki/Nottingham "Nottingham"), on the way to [Sheffield](/wiki/Sheffield "Sheffield"). In the town centre, a commemorative plaque was erected in 1988 together with a nearby tree to mark the point thought once to be the centre of Sherwood Forest. The plaque was refurbished in 2005 and moved to a ground-plinth.[[25]](#cite_note-MDC_trail-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

### Tudor and Stuart periods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Tudor and Stuart periods")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/The_Swan_Public_House.jpg/250px-The_Swan_Public_House.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Swan_Public_House.jpg)

The Swan, Church Street, dated to 1490 and rebuilt in the 16th century

In 1516, during the reign of [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII "Henry VIII"), an act of parliament settled the Manor to [Thomas Howard, 3rd Duke of Norfolk](/wiki/Thomas_Howard,_3rd_Duke_of_Norfolk "Thomas Howard, 3rd Duke of Norfolk").[[28]](#cite_note-auto1-28)

Dame Cecily Flogan in 1521, gave extensive land to the parish church and community in Mansfield in her will. The church at the time was in the hands of [Edward VI](/wiki/Edward_VI "Edward VI").[[28]](#cite_note-auto1-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Travellers in the 16th and 17th centuries had several inns and stable yards dating from the medieval period to stop at: the Harte; the Swan, which survives and has a 1490 dating stone; the Talbot; the White Bear; the Ram, with timber from before 1500; and the White Lion. Several timber-framed [cruck buildings](/wiki/Cruck "Cruck") were demolished in 1929; and in 1973 a local historical society documented another during demolition dated to 1400 or earlier. Other Tudor houses in Stockwell Gate, Bridge Street, and Lime Tree Place were also demolished to make way for development before they could be viewed for listing. Most remaining buildings are from the 17th century. The Swan was rebuilt in 1584, and became a coaching inn in the 1820's/30s.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

The Manor was passed to [George Talbot, 6th Earl of Shrewsbury](/wiki/George_Talbot,_6th_Earl_of_Shrewsbury "George Talbot, 6th Earl of Shrewsbury") the husband of [Bess of Hardwick, Countess of Shrewsbury](/wiki/Bess_of_Hardwick "Bess of Hardwick") in 1589, who then passed it to [Gilbert Talbot, 7th Earl of Shrewsbury](/wiki/Gilbert_Talbot,_7th_Earl_of_Shrewsbury "Gilbert Talbot, 7th Earl of Shrewsbury") (the 6th Earls son) until his death in 1616. Bess's daughter [Mary Talbot, Countess of Shrewsbury](/wiki/Mary_Talbot,_Countess_of_Shrewsbury "Mary Talbot, Countess of Shrewsbury") the wife of Gilbert Talbot became the owner.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Finally the Manor was passed to the [Dukes of Newcastle](/wiki/Dukes_of_Newcastle "Dukes of Newcastle") and [Portland](/wiki/Earl_of_Portland "Earl of Portland").[[28]](#cite_note-auto1-28)

Mansfield and surrounding areas in Nottinghamshire became a strong centre for [Nonconformism](/wiki/Nonconformist_(Protestantism) "Nonconformist (Protestantism)"), separating from the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England").

In 1647, [George Fox](/wiki/George_Fox "George Fox") who was originally from [Fenny Drayton](/wiki/Fenny_Drayton "Fenny Drayton") in [Leicestershire](/wiki/Leicestershire "Leicestershire") lived in Mansfield and worked as a shoemaker for 4 years. George lived in a cottage at the site of [St Phillip Neri Catholic Church](/wiki/St_Philip_Neri_Church,_Mansfield "St Philip Neri Church, Mansfield") and ground on Chesterfield Road.[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34) It was at this time he started his ministry. George Fox in 1649 was imprisoned in [Nottingham](/wiki/Nottingham "Nottingham") for interrupting the service at the church in Mansfield Woodhouse. He became the founder of the (Religious Society of Friends) [Quakers](/wiki/Quaker "Quaker"). Mansfield became the birth place of the Quaker religion after Fox had a revelation walking past [St Peter and St Paul's Church](/wiki/St_Peter_and_St_Paul%27s_Church,_Mansfield "St Peter and St Paul's Church, Mansfield") and felt compelled to preach to others. The revelation is mentioned in his journal to which he states 'and as I was walking by the steeplehouse side, in the town of Mansfield the Lord said unto me, that which people do trample upon must be thy food. And as the Lord spoke he opened it to me how that people and professors did trample upon the life, even the life of Christ was trampled upon…'. The 'steeplhouses' meaning the church of St Peter and St Pauls Church.[[35]](#cite_note-35) This was during the time of the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War").

There is a Quaker Heritage Trail in the town. The former meeting house was on the site of the bus station.[[36]](#cite_note-QHer-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38) Fox met [Elizabeth Hooton](/wiki/Elizabeth_Hooton "Elizabeth Hooton") at her home Quaker House in nearby [Skegby](/wiki/Skegby "Skegby"); she is usually considered to be the first person to accept the doctrines of Quakerism.[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Old_Meeting_House%2C_Mansfield_%285%29.jpg/250px-Old_Meeting_House%2C_Mansfield_%285%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_Meeting_House,_Mansfield_(5).jpg)

The Old Meeting House, Meeting House Yard built in 1702

The Old Meeting House (Unitarian church) on Stockwell Gate was built in 1702 and is the oldest nonconformist place of worship in Nottinghamshire. The history of the church is traced back to 1666. During the persecution of Presbyterian ministers (at the time of the [Nonconformists Act 1665](/wiki/Nonconformists_Act_1665 "Nonconformists Act 1665")), eight ministers sought refuge in Mansfield under the protection of Reverend John Firth.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

In 1690, during the reign of [William III](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England") and Queen [Mary](/wiki/Mary_II_of_England "Mary II of England"), Daniel Clay was put in the [pillory](/wiki/Pillory "Pillory") in Mansfield for disloyalty, for speaking these words: "God dam King William and Queen Mary and yt King James both should and would come again."[[42]](#cite_note-42)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Heath%27s_Almshouses_And_Adjoining_Boundary_Wall%2C_Nottingham_Road%2C_Mansfield_%285%29.jpg/250px-Heath%27s_Almshouses_And_Adjoining_Boundary_Wall%2C_Nottingham_Road%2C_Mansfield_%285%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Heath%27s_Almshouses_And_Adjoining_Boundary_Wall,_Nottingham_Road,_Mansfield_(5).jpg)

The Almshouses, Nottingham Road, founded in 1691 and rebuilt in 1855

Elizabeth Heath founded the almshouses for the poor in 1691. Six were to house Quakers and six members of the established church.[[36]](#cite_note-QHer-36)[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)

### 18th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 18th century")]

In 1709, [Samuel Brunt](/wiki/Samuel_Brunt "Samuel Brunt") left £436.15 to the relief of the poor inhabitants of Mansfield. Faith Clerkson in 1725 and Charles Thompson in 1784 both donated money to educating children in Mansfield. This formulated the beginning of the Brunt's Charity.[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Waverley_House%2C_Mansfield%2C_Old_Market_Cross_%281%29.jpg/250px-Waverley_House%2C_Mansfield%2C_Old_Market_Cross_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Waverley_House,_Mansfield,_Old_Market_Cross_(1).jpg)

Waverley House, a Grade II\* Listed Building dating to 1754

[Robert Dodsley](/wiki/Robert_Dodsley "Robert Dodsley"), who wrote *The King and the Miller of Mansfield*, was a stocking weaver in the town. His writings were set in the town also. He became one of the foremost publishers of that day, publishing Dr [Samual Johnson’s](/wiki/Samuel_Johnson "Samuel Johnson") 'London' in 1738. Later, he suggested and helped finance Johnson's *[Dictionary of the English Language](/wiki/Dictionary_of_the_English_Language "Dictionary of the English Language")*.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

In 1750, [George Whitefield](/wiki/George_Whitefield "George Whitefield"), one of the founders of [Methodism](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism")) came to preach in the town.[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49) The [Moot Hall](/wiki/Moot_Hall,_Mansfield "Moot Hall, Mansfield") in the Mansfield Market Place was erected in 1752 by [Henrietta Harley, Countess of Oxford and Countess Mortimer](/wiki/Henrietta_Harley,_Countess_of_Oxford_and_Countess_Mortimer "Henrietta Harley, Countess of Oxford and Countess Mortimer").[[50]](#cite_note-50)

The Earldom of Mansfield in Nottingham (Mansfield, Nottinghamshire) was created in 1776 for the Scottish Lawyer [William Murray](/wiki/William_Murray,_1st_Earl_of_Mansfield "William Murray, 1st Earl of Mansfield") who became the first [Earl of Mansfield](/wiki/Earl_of_Mansfield "Earl of Mansfield"). He became the Lord Chief Justice of the Kings Bench.[[51]](#cite_note-51) William Murray presided over the 1772 Somerset v Stewart case which lead to the abolition of slavery on British soil.[[52]](#cite_note-52) Lord Mansfield was a family connection of [Jane Austen’s](/wiki/Jane_Austen "Jane Austen") family. It is believed this led to the novel [Mansfield Park](/wiki/Mansfield_Park "Mansfield Park").[[53]](#cite_note-53)

It was recorded that the Mansfield [Workhouse](/wiki/Workhouse "Workhouse") was originally based on Nottingham Road in 1777, housing 56 inmates. It later moved to Stockwell Gate, where the Mansfield Union Workhouse was designed to house 300 people under the Mansfield Poor Law.[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55)

In 1790, John Throsby described Mansfield as 'a flourishing and genteel market town, general well built.....and is certainly an ancient place, and some think of high antiquity'.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 19th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Probate_Office%2C_Market_Place%2C_Mansfield_%28Now_Wetherspoons%29_%281%29.jpg/250px-Probate_Office%2C_Market_Place%2C_Mansfield_%28Now_Wetherspoons%29_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Probate_Office,_Market_Place,_Mansfield_(Now_Wetherspoons)_(1).jpg)

Former County Court built in 1867, it is now a public house

In 1851, [Lord George Bentinck](/wiki/Lord_George_Bentinck "Lord George Bentinck") was commemorated in the Cavendish Monument (Bentinck Memorial) in the Market Place in Mansfield., paid for by [public subscription](/wiki/Public_subscription "Public subscription").[[57]](#cite_note-57) The monument has a square base and three steps, and the style is [Gothic revival](/wiki/Gothic_revival "Gothic revival") It was originally intended to include a figure of Lord George, but there were insufficient funds.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

In 1894, William Horner Groves described Mansfield as "one of the quaintest and most healthy of the towns in the Midland counties, is the market town for an agricultural district of eight miles around it. It is the capital of the Broxtowe Hundred of Nottinghamshire, and gives its name to a Parliamentary Division of the county"[[18]](#cite_note-William_Horner_Groves_1894-18)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 20th century")]

The Carnegie Old Library on Leeming Street was funded and erected in 1905 by the [industrialist](/wiki/Industrialist "Industrialist") and [philanthropist](/wiki/Philanthropist "Philanthropist")
[Andrew Carnegie](/wiki/Andrew_Carnegie "Andrew Carnegie").[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60) 1905 was also the year that the [Mansfield and District Light Railways](/wiki/Mansfield_and_District_Light_Railways "Mansfield and District Light Railways") tram system was opened; it closed in 1932.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Carnegie_Old_Library%2C_Mansfield.jpg/250px-Carnegie_Old_Library%2C_Mansfield.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carnegie_Old_Library,_Mansfield.jpg)

Carnegie Old Library, now an arts centre

[Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") and her husband [Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh](/wiki/Prince_Philip,_Duke_of_Edinburgh "Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh") visited Mansfield in 1977, to mark her [Silver Jubilee](/wiki/Silver_Jubilee_of_Elizabeth_II "Silver Jubilee of Elizabeth II").[[62]](#cite_note-62)

### Ancient markets

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Ancient markets")]

Mansfield is a [market town](/wiki/Market_town "Market town") with a 700-year-old market tradition; a [royal charter](/wiki/Royal_charter "Royal charter") was issued in 1227. The present market square was created after demolition under the Mansfield Improvement Act 1823([4 Geo. 4](/wiki/4_Geo._4 "4 Geo. 4"). c. xcii).[[19]](#cite_note-Guide-19) In the centre is the Bentinck Memorial, built in 1849, which commemorates [Lord George Bentinck](/wiki/Lord_George_Bentinck "Lord George Bentinck") (1802–1848), son of the [William Bentinck, 4th Duke of Portland](/wiki/William_Bentinck,_4th_Duke_of_Portland "William Bentinck, 4th Duke of Portland"), a local landowner.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Buttercross_Westgate_Mansfield.jpg/250px-Buttercross_Westgate_Mansfield.jpg)](/wiki/File:Buttercross_Westgate_Mansfield.jpg)

Buttercoss, West Gate

The nearby Buttercross Market in West Gate, site of an old cattle market and named for the [buttercross](/wiki/Buttercross "Buttercross"), has a centrepiece of local sandstone dating from the 16th century.[[25]](#cite_note-MDC_trail-25) Mansfield District Council closed this section in 2015.[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65) Adjacent is Mansfield Library, officially opened by [Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_II "Queen Elizabeth II") in 1977 and refurbished in 2012.[[66]](#cite_note-66) The West Gate Pump commemorates John Adams bringing the first [Methodist](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism") service to Mansfield in 1788.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Geography")]

Mansfield has an average elevation of 117m with elements elevated at 197m.[[68]](#cite_note-68) The town lies on the River Maun.

Mansfield is 11.9 miles (19.2km) from [Chesterfield](/wiki/Chesterfield,_Derbyshire "Chesterfield, Derbyshire"),
15 miles (24km) from [Nottingham](/wiki/Nottingham "Nottingham"), 23.4 miles (37.6km) from [Derby](/wiki/Derby "Derby") and 30.6 miles (49.2km) from [Sheffield](/wiki/Sheffield "Sheffield").

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Climate")]

Mansfield has a [temperate oceanic climate](/wiki/Temperate_oceanic_climate "Temperate oceanic climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): Cfb), with a narrow temperature range, an even spread of rainfall, low levels of sunshine and often breezy conditions throughout the year. The closest weather-station records for Mansfield come from Warsop in [Meden Vale](/wiki/Meden_Vale "Meden Vale"), seven miles to the north.

The absolute maximum temperature record for the area stands at 34.6 °C (94.3 °F), recorded in August 1990.[[69]](#cite_note-eca.knmi.nl-69) In a typical year the warmest day should reach 28.9 °C (84.0 °F) and 12.72 days should reach 25.1 °C (77.2 °F) or higher.[[70]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-70)[[71]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-71)

The absolute minimum temperature record for the area is −19.1 °C (−2.4 °F), recorded in January 1987. There is air frost on an average of 59 nights a year.[[72]](#cite_note-ReferenceC-72)

Rainfall averages 634 mm a year, with 113 days reporting in excess of 1 mm of rain (observation period 1971–2000).[[73]](#cite_note-ReferenceD-73)[[74]](#cite_note-ReferenceE-74)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Economy")]

### Town centre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Town centre")]

Mansfield has a large [market place](/wiki/Market_place "Market place") within its commercial and retail centre.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Queens_Walk_Mansfield.jpg/250px-Queens_Walk_Mansfield.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queens_Walk_Mansfield.jpg)

Queens Walk

Until 2016, there was also market trading at the old Buttercross Market.[[76]](#cite_note-77)[[77]](#cite_note-78)[[78]](#cite_note-79)[[79]](#cite_note-80) Among Mansfield's retail outlets is the Four Seasons [shopping centre](/wiki/Shopping_mall "Shopping mall") created in 1973–1976, with over 50 units occupied by national chains and phone shops.[[80]](#cite_note-81)[[81]](#cite_note-82)[[82]](#cite_note-FourSeasonsGuide-83)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Exchange_Row%2C_Mansfield.jpg/250px-Exchange_Row%2C_Mansfield.jpg)](/wiki/File:Exchange_Row,_Mansfield.jpg)

Exchange Row

Close to the Market Place is Leeming Street, which houses [Mansfield Museum](/wiki/Mansfield_Museum "Mansfield Museum"), [The Palace Theatre](/wiki/Palace_Theatre,_Mansfield "Palace Theatre, Mansfield"), restaurants, public houses, bars and night clubs.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Westgate%2C_Mansfield.jpg/250px-Westgate%2C_Mansfield.jpg)](/wiki/File:Westgate,_Mansfield.jpg)

Westgate

There are also three outdoor retail parks, two with adjacent branded fast-food outlets.[[83]](#cite_note-84)[[84]](#cite_note-85)[[85]](#cite_note-86)

Since 2010, there has been a town-centre [Business Improvement District](/wiki/Business_improvement_district#United_Kingdom "Business improvement district") (BID), financed by 2 per cent extra on the rateable value of nearby businesses.[[86]](#cite_note-Report2011-87)[[87]](#cite_note-88)[[88]](#cite_note-Council_report_2014-89)[[89]](#cite_note-90)

The BID also offers events to attract visitors and raise awareness, provides security including banning orders and improved shop frontages,[[90]](#cite_note-BID_Promotional-91)[[91]](#cite_note-mansfieldbid.com-92) Other BID moves have been "gating off" [alleyways](/wiki/Alley "Alley") blighted by anti-social behaviour, improving signage, and enhancing cleansing operations.[[92]](#cite_note-93)[[93]](#cite_note-94)[[94]](#cite_note-95)[[95]](#cite_note-96) and in 2013 installed a [crowd-funded](/wiki/Crowd-funded "Crowd-funded") town centre [Wi-Fi](/wiki/Hotspot_(Wi-Fi) "Hotspot (Wi-Fi)") internet installation costing £37,000.[[96]](#cite_note-97)

In 2012, Mansfield Constituency Labour Party criticised the BID for receiving almost a million pounds in its first three years, with little to show for it.[[97]](#cite_note-98)

### Regeneration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Regeneration")]

Mansfield is going through a period of [urban regeneration](/wiki/Urban_regeneration "Urban regeneration"), with new homes being built for a growing demand. Data collated by the [Office for National Statistics](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics") in 2020 advised that more people are moving from London to Mansfield than any other part of Nottinghamshire.[[98]](#cite_note-99)

Mansfield District Council received £25 million from the UK Government's Levelling Up Fund in 2023 for the Mansfield Connect project, which aims to regenerate the former [Co-operative](/wiki/Co-operatives_UK "Co-operatives UK") department store (taken over by [Beales](/wiki/Beales_(department_store) "Beales (department store)") in 2011)[[99]](#cite_note-100) into a multi-agency and community hub for the NHS, the Department of Work and Pensions, Nottinghamshire County Council, Vision West Nottinghamshire College and Mansfield CVS.[[100]](#cite_note-101)

In February 2022 [Severn Trent Water](/wiki/Severn_Trent_Water "Severn Trent Water") shared its £76 million Green Recovery Project for flood alleviation investment for the town, including rain garden areas around the Market Place, a memorial garden at the back of the Old Town Hall and a [pocket park](/wiki/Pocket_park "Pocket park") on the corner of Walkden Street/Quaker Way.[[101]](#cite_note-102) Some of the £76 million was spent on the memorial garden on Exchange Row, landscaped areas in Mansfield Market Place and Market House Place as part of the Sustainable Urban Drainage System programme to prevent flooding.[[102]](#cite_note-103)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/The_memorial_garden%2C_Queen_Street.jpg/250px-The_memorial_garden%2C_Queen_Street.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_memorial_garden,_Queen_Street.jpg)

The Memorial Garden, Exchange Row, part of the Green Recovery Project

Also in 2023, the council purchased White Hart Street in the town centre and announced plans for its redevelopment[[103]](#cite_note-104) — a mixed-use development of commercial premises and affordable homes was given the green light in 2025, and the scheme is scheduled for completion in 2028.[[104]](#cite_note-105) This is part of the Mansfield Town Centre Masterplan.[[105]](#cite_note-106)

A significant number of new homes and developments have been built or are planned in Mansfield, including High Oakham Park[[106]](#cite_note-107) and the Lindhurst development, which is to include 1700 homes, a hotel, health centre, primary school, care home and offices.[[107]](#cite_note-108)

Rosemary Centre, built as a large [weaving shed](/wiki/Weaving_shed "Weaving shed") in 1907 by John Harwood Cash and converted to retail in 1984,[[108]](#cite_note-109) is a pedestrianised area off the town centre with a covered streetside parade. In April 2023, a planning application to demolish the Rosemary Centre to build a Lidl supermarket and another retail unit was approved. Demolition of the centre began in January 2026 and is yet to be completed.[[109]](#cite_note-110)[[110]](#cite_note-111) The adjacent Walkden Street Car Park's top floor collapsed, and Mansfield District Council have decided for the car park to be demolished also.[[111]](#cite_note-112) This again forms part of the town centre Masterplan.[[112]](#cite_note-113)

A new hotel is expected to be built on the former bus station as planned from 2020 at the cost of £12 million. Planning permission has been granted but due to the rising costs of inflation the plans are being revised to be cost effective.[[113]](#cite_note-114)

### Civic Centre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Civic Centre")]

The headquarters of the [Mansfield District Council](/wiki/Mansfield_District "Mansfield District") at Chesterfield Road South were purpose-built in 1986, bringing together workers from 12 offices across the district. The project took two years and over-ran the anticipated cost by £1 million, totalling £6.7 million, then the council's biggest spending scheme.[[114]](#cite_note-115) It was opened in 1987 by [Princess Anne](/wiki/Princess_Anne "Princess Anne").[[115]](#cite_note-Annals-116) In-house catering facilities are run by outside contractors.[[116]](#cite_note-117) The civic centre includes Job Centre Plus, an agency within the [Department for Work and Pensions](/wiki/Department_for_Work_and_Pensions "Department for Work and Pensions").[[117]](#cite_note-118)

Mansfield Community Partnership at the Civic Centre is a centralised hub for law and order, with police, street wardens, housing, domestic abuse and anti social behaviour officers in a dedicated town-centre unit.[[118]](#cite_note-119)[[119]](#cite_note-103.2_June_19-120)[[120]](#cite_note-121)[[121]](#cite_note-122)

In 2021, the council proposed a new community hub at the [old town hall](/wiki/Old_Town_Hall,_Mansfield "Old Town Hall, Mansfield") in the town centre, intending to relocate staff together with other parties having vested interests in the present building and area.[[122]](#cite_note-123) By 2023 the council's priorities had changed, having acquired a £20 million grant from central government towards the cost of converting the nearby old [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco "Art Deco")-styled former Co-Operative store, closed since 2020.[[123]](#cite_note-124) Named *Mansfield Connect*, a multi-agency hub is planned with space sub-let to partner organisations such as police, social care and [Jobcentre Plus](/wiki/Jobcentre_Plus "Jobcentre Plus").[[124]](#cite_note-125)

#### Regeneration history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Regeneration history")]

Reconstruction of [King's Mill Hospital](/wiki/King%27s_Mill_Hospital "King's Mill Hospital"), part of which was completed by 2009, is near the MARR road (Mansfield and Ashfield Regeneration Route) which opened in 2004,[[125]](#cite_note-126) a bypass route around the town designed to reduce traffic through-flow and improve public access by connecting the A617 at [Pleasley](/wiki/Pleasley "Pleasley") to the [A617](/wiki/A_roads_in_Zone_6_of_the_Great_Britain_numbering_scheme#Triple-digit_roads "A roads in Zone 6 of the Great Britain numbering scheme") at [Rainworth](/wiki/Rainworth "Rainworth"). In 2009 Mansfield made an unsuccessful bid for city status, appending redevelopment plans for retail, residential and leisure facilities with road improvements gradually being made.[[126]](#cite_note-127)

Several regeneration projects planned for Mansfield involved mass demolition, but the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis") and subsequent central-government funding cuts and escalating [austerity measures](/wiki/Austerity_measures "Austerity measures") deferred them. Mansfield District Council promoted two new developments: Arrival Square, opened 2008,[[127]](#cite_note-128) an office block occupied by the [Probation Service](/wiki/National_Probation_Service "National Probation Service") by the rail station;[[128]](#cite_note-129) and Queen's Place—completed in late 2013—which cost the council £2.4 million. It offered two new ground-floor retail units and six offices in Queen Street between the new [transport interchange](/wiki/Transport_interchange "Transport interchange") and the market square.[[129]](#cite_note-queens_place-130)

### The Industrial Revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: The Industrial Revolution")]

Although Mansfield itself does not show signs of coal mining, a few areas near the town still do. Coal mining was the main industry for most of the 20th century. A violent episode in the [UK miners' strike (1984–1985)](/wiki/UK_miners%27_strike_(1984%E2%80%931985) "UK miners' strike (1984–1985)") occurred in Mansfield on [May Day](/wiki/May_Day "May Day") 1984.[[130]](#cite_note-SundayTimes-131) Most of the area's miners had voted against a strike, but the local [union](/wiki/National_Union_of_Mineworkers_(Great_Britain) "National Union of Mineworkers (Great Britain)") initially maintained that the strike was official to show solidarity with strikers in other areas. When the coal board granted an extra day of leave after the bank holiday, a group of working miners confronted union officials and violence broke out with striking miners.[[130]](#cite_note-SundayTimes-131) Mansfield later hosted a breakaway union, the [Union of Democratic Mineworkers](/wiki/Union_of_Democratic_Mineworkers "Union of Democratic Mineworkers"), which recruited many who had opposed the 1984–1985 strike.[[131]](#cite_note-132) The [Coal Authority](/wiki/Coal_Authority "Coal Authority") is based in Mansfield, and the larger than lifesize statue *Tribute to the British Miner* by Nikolaos Kotziamanis was erected in 2003 to honour the town's mining heritage.[[132]](#cite_note-133)

As demand for coal fell, Mansfield's pits wound down and miners had to find other work. The [headstocks](/wiki/Headframe "Headframe") close to the village of [Clipstone](/wiki/Clipstone "Clipstone") are an important local landmark and said to be the highest in Europe.[[133]](#cite_note-campaign-134) Community groups are trying to preserve them as a reminder of the area's mining history.[[133]](#cite_note-campaign-134)[[134]](#cite_note-135)[[135]](#cite_note-136)

A few streets in and around the town form long rows of terraced houses reminiscent of the affordable housing provided for mine workers in the prime of the industry. Many were demolished in 2012 in Pleasley Hill, Market Warsop and elsewhere. New houses have been built since in this area of Pleasley Hill.[[136]](#cite_note-137)

### Mansfield Brewery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Mansfield Brewery")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Mansfield_Brewery.jpg/250px-Mansfield_Brewery.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mansfield_Brewery.jpg)

Former offices of [Mansfield Brewery](/wiki/Mansfield_Brewery "Mansfield Brewery")

[Mansfield Brewery](/wiki/Mansfield_Brewery "Mansfield Brewery") was best known for Mansfield Bitter, and its advertising slogan "not much matches Mansfield", which was later used as the title of a play by [Kevin Fegan](/wiki/Kevin_Fegan "Kevin Fegan") set in the town.[[137]](#cite_note-138) In the 1980s, the brewery ran adverts referencing the achievements of contemporary world figures such as Ronald Reagan with the slogan "but he's never had a pint of Mansfield".[[138]](#cite_note-139)[[139]](#cite_note-140) The brewery was acquired in 1999 by [Wolverhampton & Dudley Breweries](/wiki/Wolverhampton_%26_Dudley_Breweries "Wolverhampton & Dudley Breweries"),[[140]](#cite_note-brew-141) and closed in 2002. Most of the site was redeveloped as housing,[[141]](#cite_note-142) while the ornate office building 'Chadburn House' is now office space for businesses, including the local newspaper,[[142]](#cite_note-143) and a [micro brewery](/wiki/Micro_brewery "Micro brewery") with a cafe and bar.[[143]](#cite_note-144) The site is now owned by [Vision West Nottinghamshire College](/wiki/Vision_West_Nottinghamshire_College "Vision West Nottinghamshire College"), a further education college based in Mansfield, which uses it to deliver construction courses and training.

Mansfield's old-established soft drink manufacturer, R. L. Jones, with brand names *Sunecta* and *Mandora*, was bought by Mansfield Brewery in 1977.[[115]](#cite_note-Annals-116) A move to a modern factory in Bellamy Road in 1975 released land projected for a high-density housing development known as Layton Burroughs.[[115]](#cite_note-Annals-116) Mansfield Brewery sold the business in 1988 for £21.5 million to the Scottish drinks company [A. G. Barr](/wiki/A._G._Barr "A. G. Barr") plc, producer of [Irn-Bru](/wiki/Irn-Bru "Irn-Bru"), [Tizer](/wiki/Tizer "Tizer"), and Mandora.[[115]](#cite_note-Annals-116) At the time the firm employed 400 people. Production ceased there in January 2011 when A. G. Barr moved production to other sites.[[144]](#cite_note-145)[[145]](#cite_note-146)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Transport")]

### Railway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Railway")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Mansfield_Railway_Station%2C_Platform_1_%288%29.jpg/250px-Mansfield_Railway_Station%2C_Platform_1_%288%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mansfield_Railway_Station,_Platform_1_(8).jpg)

[Mansfield railway station](/wiki/Mansfield_railway_station_(England) "Mansfield railway station (England)")

[Mansfield railway station](/wiki/Mansfield_railway_station_(England) "Mansfield railway station (England)") is on the [Robin Hood Line](/wiki/Robin_Hood_Line "Robin Hood Line"), which connects the town with [Nottingham](/wiki/Nottingham_railway_station "Nottingham railway station") and [Worksop](/wiki/Worksop_railway_station "Worksop railway station"); the line was opened in 1995. Trains run generally at hourly intervals each way.[[146]](#cite_note-timetable-147)

The town was originally the terminus of the [Mansfield and Pinxton Railway](/wiki/Mansfield_and_Pinxton_Railway "Mansfield and Pinxton Railway"), a horse-drawn [plateway](/wiki/Plateway "Plateway") built in 1819 and one of the first acquisitions of the newly formed [Midland Railway](/wiki/Midland_Railway "Midland Railway").[[147]](#cite_note-ourmansfield-148) The Midland used the final section to extend its new [Leen Valley](/wiki/Leen_Valley "Leen Valley") line to the present station in 1849.

The Midland Railway extended its Rolleston Junction–Southwell branch to Mansfield in 1871; continued the line north to Worksop in 1875; opened a link from [Mansfield Woodhouse](/wiki/Mansfield_Woodhouse_railway_station "Mansfield Woodhouse railway station") to [Westhouses](/wiki/Westhouses "Westhouses") and Blackwell in 1886; and completed another link from Pleasley through Bolsover to Barrow Hill in 1890. The locally promoted Mansfield Railway, between Kirkby South Junction and Clipstone Junction, broke the Midland Railway monopoly; it was opened in stages between 1913 and 1916 for goods trains and, in 1917, for [Nottingham Victoria](/wiki/Nottingham_Victoria_railway_station "Nottingham Victoria railway station")–[Ollerton](/wiki/Ollerton_railway_station "Ollerton railway station") passenger trains, calling at a second Mansfield passenger station. Though nominally independent, the Mansfield Railway connected at both ends with the [Great Central Railway](/wiki/Great_Central_Railway "Great Central Railway"), which worked the trains.[[148]](#cite_note-149) Mansfield had two railway stations: *Mansfield Town*, the former Midland station on Station Road; and *Mansfield Central*, the former Mansfield Railway station in Great Central Road, near Ratcliffe Gate. [Mansfield & District Light Railways](/wiki/Mansfield_%26_District_Light_Railways "Mansfield & District Light Railways") ran a tram service between 1905 and 1932.

[Mansfield Central station](/wiki/Mansfield_Central_railway_station "Mansfield Central railway station") lost its scheduled passenger services at the beginning of 1956 and Mansfield Town station closed to passengers in 1964, leaving Mansfield without passenger railway service until 1995. During this period, Mansfield was, by some definitions, the largest town in Britain without a railway station.[[147]](#cite_note-ourmansfield-148) The closest station was [Alfreton](/wiki/Alfreton_railway_station "Alfreton railway station"); between 1973 and 1995, it was named 'Alfreton and Mansfield Parkway' to encourage use as a railhead for Mansfield.

The Midland Railway's 1875 [viaduct](/wiki/Viaduct "Viaduct") in White Hart Street is a Grade II listed building.[[149]](#cite_note-150)

### Road

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Road")]

The [M1 motorway](/wiki/M1_motorway "M1 motorway") lies west of Mansfield. It is 6.8 miles (10.9 km) from junction 29 at [Heath, Derbyshire](/wiki/Heath,_Derbyshire "Heath, Derbyshire") for traffic from the north and [Chesterfield](/wiki/Chesterfield,_Derbyshire "Chesterfield, Derbyshire"), and 7.7 miles (12.4 km) from junction 27 at [Annesley](/wiki/Annesley "Annesley") for traffic from the south.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/N_C_C_Mansfield_Bus_Station_%282%29.jpg/250px-N_C_C_Mansfield_Bus_Station_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:N_C_C_Mansfield_Bus_Station_(2).jpg)

[Mansfield bus station](/wiki/Mansfield_bus_station "Mansfield bus station") with the [turf-roof](/wiki/Green_roof "Green roof") of Queen's Place low-energy building visible behind

The [A60 road](/wiki/A60_road "A60 road") runs north–south through Mansfield, between [Nottingham](/wiki/Nottingham "Nottingham") and [Worksop](/wiki/Worksop "Worksop"). The [A617 road](/wiki/A617_road "A617 road") skirts around the town, providing a road link eastwards towards [Newark-on-Trent](/wiki/Newark-on-Trent "Newark-on-Trent") as well as westwards towards Chesterfield and the M1.

The [A38 road](/wiki/A38_road "A38 road"), the longest 2-digit A-road in Great Britain, terminates at Mansfield, and provides the town with a direct link to [Derby](/wiki/Derby "Derby").

### Buses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Buses")]

Buses in Mansfield are operated mainly by [Stagecoach](/wiki/Stagecoach_in_Mansfield "Stagecoach in Mansfield") from [Mansfield bus station](/wiki/Mansfield_bus_station "Mansfield bus station"), with [Trent Barton](/wiki/Trent_Barton "Trent Barton") and [National Express](/wiki/National_Express_Coaches "National Express Coaches") also working the area.

The bus station opened in 2013 near the railway station as part of the *Gateway to Mansfield* scheme,[[150]](#cite_note-Gateway-151)[[151]](#cite_note-NottsBusStation-152) replacing a 1977 bus station closer to the town centre.

## Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Politics")]

Mansfield is in [Mansfield parliamentary constituency](/wiki/Mansfield_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Mansfield (UK Parliament constituency)"), which also includes neighbouring [Warsop](/wiki/Warsop "Warsop"). [Steve Yemm](/wiki/Steve_Yemm "Steve Yemm") (Labour) has been the Member of Parliament since 2024.

Mansfield has a directly elected mayor, the [Mayor of Mansfield](/wiki/Mayor_of_Mansfield#2019 "Mayor of Mansfield"), which since 2019 has been Andy Abrahams.[[152]](#cite_note-153)

In April 2017, Sophie Whitby was elected to the Mansfield district as a [Member of Youth Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Youth_Parliament "Member of Youth Parliament"), on a manifesto that included promoting equality for the [LGBT](/wiki/LGBT "LGBT") community.[[153]](#cite_note-154)

## Places of interest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Places of interest")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Mansfield_Museum_1.jpg/250px-Mansfield_Museum_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mansfield_Museum_1.jpg)

[Mansfield Museum](/wiki/Mansfield_Museum "Mansfield Museum"), Leeming Street

[Mansfield Museum](/wiki/Mansfield_Museum "Mansfield Museum"), is situated on Leeming Street. The museum opened in 1904.[[154]](#cite_note-155) and has been based on its present site since 1938.

The Quaker Heritage Trail, starts from [Mansfield Bus Station](/wiki/Mansfield_Bus_Station "Mansfield Bus Station"). A map and audio guide is found on the Nottinghamshire County Council website.[[155]](#cite_note-156)

The Mansfield Heritage Trail starts from the museum and this comes with a map and audio guide.[[156]](#cite_note-157)

[St Peter and St Paul's Church](/wiki/St_Peter_and_St_Paul%27s_Church,_Mansfield "St Peter and St Paul's Church, Mansfield") is a [Grade I listed building](/wiki/Listed_building "Listed building"). The church is mentioned in the Doomsday Book.[[24]](#cite_note-auto-24)

The viaduct was built in 1875.[[157]](#cite_note-158) And it is grade II listed.[[158]](#cite_note-159)

The Old Meeting House built in 1702.

[Kings Mill Reservoir](/wiki/Kings_Mill_Reservoir "Kings Mill Reservoir") built in 1839.

## Parks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Parks")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Titchfield_Park%2C_Mansfield_-_geograph.org.uk_-_535910.jpg/250px-Titchfield_Park%2C_Mansfield_-_geograph.org.uk_-_535910.jpg)](/wiki/File:Titchfield_Park,_Mansfield_-_geograph.org.uk_-_535910.jpg)

Titchfield Park and [River Maun](/wiki/River_Maun "River Maun")

Titchfield Park, on the same site as the Water Meadows swimming complex, offers large grassy areas on both sides of the [River Maun](/wiki/River_Maun "River Maun"), crossed by two footbridges. It has a bowls green, hard tennis courts, a basketball court, a children's play area, and many flowerbeds.

Fisher Lane Park stretches from the top of Littleworth to Rock Hill. It is popular with dog walkers and kite flyers, and since the installation of a concrete skate plaza, with skaters. In the summer, children's rides and stalls are set up in the park.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Carr_Bank_Park%2C_Windmill_Lane%2C_Mansfield_%2829%29.jpg/250px-Carr_Bank_Park%2C_Windmill_Lane%2C_Mansfield_%2829%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carr_Bank_Park,_Windmill_Lane,_Mansfield_(29).jpg)

Carr Bank Park

Carr Bank Park has a rocky grotto, a bandstand and summer flower beds. It has a war memorial built of local [sandstone](/wiki/Sandstone "Sandstone"), dedicated to soldiers killed in action since the end of the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), to complement the original setting unveiled after the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War") in 1921.[[159]](#cite_note-160)

Other parks in Mansfield are Berry Hill and King George V Parks.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Entrance_to_King_George_V_Playing_Field_-_geograph.org.uk_-_7306213.jpg/250px-Entrance_to_King_George_V_Playing_Field_-_geograph.org.uk_-_7306213.jpg)](/wiki/File:Entrance_to_King_George_V_Playing_Field_-_geograph.org.uk_-_7306213.jpg)

King George V Park

Mansfield is a few miles from [Sherwood Forest](/wiki/Sherwood_Forest "Sherwood Forest"), a Royal Forest famous for its links with [Robin Hood](/wiki/Robin_Hood "Robin Hood"). Mansfield used to have a tree and a plaque mounted on a plinth in West Gate to mark what was the centre of Sherwood Forest. Nearby was a giant metallic feather sculpture dating from 2007. This was named *[A Spire for Mansfield](/wiki/A_Spire_for_Mansfield "A Spire for Mansfield")*.

## Entertainment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Entertainment")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Palace_Theatre%2C_Leeming_Street%2C_Mansfield_%28Previously_the_Civic_Theatre%29_%282%29.jpg/250px-Palace_Theatre%2C_Leeming_Street%2C_Mansfield_%28Previously_the_Civic_Theatre%29_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palace_Theatre,_Leeming_Street,_Mansfield_(Previously_the_Civic_Theatre)_(2).jpg)

[Palace Theatre](/wiki/Palace_Theatre,_Mansfield "Palace Theatre, Mansfield")

The [Palace Theatre](/wiki/Palace_Theatre,_Mansfield "Palace Theatre, Mansfield") in Leeming St is the town's prime entertainment venue. Built as a cinema in 1910 and originally known as the Palace Electric Theatre, it was adapted into a live theatre with a [proscenium arch](/wiki/Proscenium_arch "Proscenium arch").[[160]](#cite_note-161) It was known as the Civic Hall and Civic Theatre before the current name was revived in 1995.[[115]](#cite_note-Annals-116) With a [seating capacity](/wiki/Seating_capacity "Seating capacity") of 534, the theatre is a mid-scale touring venue.[[161]](#cite_note-162)[[162]](#cite_note-163) It presents a programme of professional and amateur productions and a yearly [pantomime](/wiki/Pantomime "Pantomime").[[163]](#cite_note-164)[[164]](#cite_note-165)[[165]](#cite_note-166)

Mansfield Super Bowl, a 28-lane alley with hospitality, opened in 1991. Facing closure in 2014, it was sold and refurbished in 2015.[[166]](#cite_note-167)

The old [Carnegie library](/wiki/Carnegie_library "Carnegie library"), founded in 1905 in Leeming Street, became an arts and performance centre in 1976.[[167]](#cite_note-168) It houses a recording studio, meeting room and 100-seat Studio Theatre.[[168]](#cite_note-169)

Mansfield also has a large [Odeon Cinemas](/wiki/Odeon_Cinemas "Odeon Cinemas") on a new retail and entertainment park outside the town centre.[[169]](#cite_note-170) The previous [ABC](/wiki/ABC_Cinemas "ABC Cinemas") town-centre cinema was used as a [snooker centre](/wiki/Snooker "Snooker") until closure in 2012;[[170]](#cite_note-171) late in 2013 it was converted into a church.[[171]](#cite_note-172)

[Odeon Cinema](/wiki/Odeon_Cinemas "Odeon Cinemas") and restaurants/bar.

### Summer in the Streets

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Summer in the Streets")]

Every year between June and August, Mansfield District Council hosts a *Summer in the Streets* festival. This consists of various public events held all across the town over many days, such as children's entertainment, fairground rides in the market square, and hands-on workshops for things like crafts and circus skills.

The festival highlight is a final event in Titchfield Park called Party in the Park. Its range of entertainment includes live music acts by local bands, performances from local dance groups, and activities such as face painting. For 2012 and 2013, this culminating event was cancelled for austerity reasons.[[172]](#cite_note-173)[[173]](#cite_note-174)

### Entertainment history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Entertainment history")]

Mansfield was home to Venue 44,[[174]](#cite_note-175) a nightclub that gave birth to the superclub [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance_(club) "Renaissance (club)") which was operated there in 1992–1994 by Geoff Oakes[[175]](#cite_note-176) and launched the DJ's [Sasha](/wiki/Sasha_(DJ) "Sasha (DJ)"), [John Digweed](/wiki/John_Digweed "John Digweed"), Nigel Dawson[[176]](#cite_note-177) and Ian Ossia to global fame.[[177]](#cite_note-178) The building was demolished in 2010.

The Intake, a live-music venue in Kirkland Avenue, closed in 2016.[[178]](#cite_note-179) The [Town Mill](/wiki/Town_Mill,_Mansfield "Town Mill, Mansfield"), a former waterside mill on the banks of the Maun at the edge of the town centre, was turned into a pub and live music venue in 2002, but closed in 2010, citing the smoking ban, rising beer prices and recession among its reasons for failure.[[179]](#cite_note-180)

On 21 August 2010, the Irish boy band [Westlife](/wiki/Westlife "Westlife") performed live at [Field Mill](/wiki/Field_Mill "Field Mill") stadium, home to Mansfield's football team, [Mansfield Town F.C.](/wiki/Mansfield_Town_F.C. "Mansfield Town F.C.")

## Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Religion")]

[St Peter and St Paul's Church](/wiki/St_Peter_and_St_Paul%27s_Church,_Mansfield "St Peter and St Paul's Church, Mansfield") is a [Grade I listed building](/wiki/Listed_building "Listed building"). It is mentioned in the [Domesday Book](/wiki/Domesday_Book "Domesday Book") in 1086 and was mostly built by the [Normans](/wiki/Normans "Normans").[[24]](#cite_note-auto-24)

[St Johns Church](/wiki/St_John%27s_Church,_Mansfield "St John's Church, Mansfield"), a Grade II listed building, was built in 1854 and designed by [Henry Isaac Stevens](/wiki/Henry_Isaac_Stevens "Henry Isaac Stevens").[[180]](#cite_note-181)[[181]](#cite_note-182) [St Mark's Church](/wiki/St_Mark%27s_Church,_Mansfield "St Mark's Church, Mansfield") was built in 1897; the church building is Grade II listed.[[182]](#cite_note-183) St Lawrence the Martyr Church on Skerry Hill was built in 1909 and is Grade II listed.[[183]](#cite_note-184)[[184]](#cite_note-185)[[185]](#cite_note-186)[[186]](#cite_note-187)

[St Philip Neri Church](/wiki/St_Philip_Neri_Church,_Mansfield "St Philip Neri Church, Mansfield") is a Roman Catholic Church on Chesterfield Road South.[[187]](#cite_note-188)

A [Quaker Meeting House](/wiki/Quaker_Meeting_House "Quaker Meeting House") of the [Religious Society of Friends](/wiki/Religious_Society_of_Friends "Religious Society of Friends") is on Rosemary Street.[[188]](#cite_note-189)[[189]](#cite_note-190)

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Sport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/One_Call_Stadium.jpg/250px-One_Call_Stadium.jpg)](/wiki/File:One_Call_Stadium.jpg)

[One Call Stadium](/wiki/Field_Mill "Field Mill"), home of [Mansfield Town FC](/wiki/Mansfield_Town_FC "Mansfield Town FC")

Mansfield is home to [Mansfield Town FC](/wiki/Mansfield_Town_FC "Mansfield Town FC"), known as the Stags or yellows. Relegated to the [Conference National](/wiki/Conference_National "Conference National") after 77 years in the [Football League](/wiki/Football_League "Football League") at the end of the 2007–2008 season, Mansfield Town returned to the Football League after winning the 2012–2013 Conference National title. They were promoted to League One (Third Tier) for the first time in 22 years in April 2024.[[190]](#cite_note-191) [Non-League](/wiki/Non-League "Non-League") club [AFC Mansfield](/wiki/AFC_Mansfield "AFC Mansfield") plays in the [Forest Town](/wiki/Forest_Town,_Nottinghamshire "Forest Town, Nottinghamshire") area of Mansfield.

[Mansfield Rugby Club](/wiki/Mansfield_Rugby_Club "Mansfield Rugby Club") is a [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") club based at Eakring Road and currently plays in [Midlands 1 East](/wiki/Midlands_1_East "Midlands 1 East"), a sixth-tier league in the [English rugby union system](/wiki/English_rugby_union_system "English rugby union system"). It won the Notts Cup for five years in succession and for a record 18 times.

[Mansfield Giants](/wiki/Mansfield_Giants "Mansfield Giants") is Mansfield's Premier Basketball Club, and has a three-star Accreditation and Club Mark from the English Sports Council. The team plays in the [England Basketball](/wiki/England_Basketball "England Basketball") (EB2).

Mansfield hosted an annual [half marathon](/wiki/Half_marathon "Half marathon") for more than 30 years until 2011.

[Angling](/wiki/Angling "Angling") is well supported in the Mansfield district, where ponds remain from the former textile milling industry.

[Tennis](/wiki/Tennis "Tennis") is catered for by Mansfield Lawn Tennis Club located at the same site since 1883, with two grass courts and four [asphalt](/wiki/Asphalt_concrete "Asphalt concrete") courts, three of them floodlit.[[191]](#cite_note-192) Further hard-surface courts are found in the district at six Mansfield District Council park locations.[[192]](#cite_note-193)

Mansfield is home to Mansfield Roller Derby, Mansfield's premier Flat Track [Roller Derby](/wiki/Roller_Derby "Roller Derby") league.[[193]](#cite_note-194)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Rebecca_Adlington_Swimming_Centre_%286%29.jpg/250px-Rebecca_Adlington_Swimming_Centre_%286%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rebecca_Adlington_Swimming_Centre_(6).jpg)

Rebecca Adlington Swimming Centre

Mansfield has two indoor swimming centres and a third, smaller pool attached to a school.[[194]](#cite_note-195) These facilities give Mansfield the largest square meterage of indoor water-sports facilities per capita of any town in the United Kingdom with less than 100,000 inhabitants.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The town is one of three outlets for the [Nottinghamshire County Council](/wiki/Nottinghamshire_County_Council "Nottinghamshire County Council") Swim Squad, which competes as Nova Centurion. The Rebecca Adlington Swimming Centre at Sherwood Swimming Baths includes a 25-metre pool and an endless stroke-improvement training pool with variable-resistance water flow. The complex uses a ground-source heat pump backed by a biomass boiler burning wood pellets prepared from waste by a local wood yard.[[195]](#cite_note-196)[[196]](#cite_note-197)

At the [Beijing 2008 Olympic Games](/wiki/Beijing_2008_Olympic_Games "Beijing 2008 Olympic Games"), a Mansfield contestant, [Rebecca Adlington](/wiki/Rebecca_Adlington "Rebecca Adlington"), won two gold medals, for 400 and 800-metre freestyle swimming. After her record-breaking success, Adlington was welcomed home to Mansfield by thousands lining the streets to applaud as she passed in an open top bus. This culminated in an appearance at the [old Town Hall](/wiki/Old_Town_Hall,_Mansfield "Old Town Hall, Mansfield") in the Market Square. Her success boosted swimming interest in the area, leading to expansion of swimming classes to encourage young people to begin swimming. At the [2012 Olympic Games](/wiki/2012_Olympic_Games "2012 Olympic Games") in and around London, Adlington won two Bronze medals again for 400 and 800 metres, the best performance of a generally disappointing *Team GB* swimming squad. She retired from competitive swimming in February 2013.[[197]](#cite_note-198)

Water Meadows swimming complex in Bath Street, on the site of the former Mansfield Baths, has a gym and a soft-play area for children with an adjoining café, as well as one 25-metre competition pool, two other pools, and a small teaching pool. The leisure lagoon pool has an [artificial wave](/wiki/Artificial_wave "Artificial wave") machine operating periodically, and also a slide and a shallow area like a beach. The complex is popular with family groups, and many surrounding schools make use of its facilities.

Mansfield Bowling Club is reputed to have origins in the 1700s. The club played at a [bowling green](/wiki/Bowling_green "Bowling green") to the rear of the Bowl in Hand pub in the town centre, until relocating into the grounds of [Queen Elizabeth's Academy](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth%27s_Academy "Queen Elizabeth's Academy"), with a new facility including pavilion opening in 2009.[[198]](#cite_note-199)[[199]](#cite_note-200)

## Cemeteries and crematorium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Cemeteries and crematorium")]

The main cemetery and crematorium occupy a 10 acres (4.0 hectares) site accessed from Derby Road, on the southern edge of town near the boundary with Ashfield.[[200]](#cite_note-MDC_cemetery-201) They share a car park. In late 2015, Mansfield District Council recognised the need for additional spaces and planning consent was obtained.[[201]](#cite_note-202) The older part of the cemetery, fronting Nottingham Road and Forest Hill (the old Derby Road) has on-street parking. Site access on foot can be hard due to the steep slope.[[200]](#cite_note-MDC_cemetery-201)

The cemetery was opened in 1857 due to insufficient church graveyard space,[[200]](#cite_note-MDC_cemetery-201) the mid-to-late Victorian population growth and several then-new churches built with little or no dedicated graveyard areas.[[202]](#cite_note-203)[[203]](#cite_note-204)[[204]](#cite_note-205) A 10-acre extension was made in 1898.[[200]](#cite_note-MDC_cemetery-201) Registered by the [Commonwealth War Graves Commission](/wiki/Commonwealth_War_Graves_Commission "Commonwealth War Graves Commission") as 'Nottingham Road Cemetery', this cemetery contains the war graves of 51 Commonwealth service personnel of World War I and 45 from World War II.[[205]](#cite_note-206)

The adjacent *Mansfield and District Crematorium*, with two chapels seating 35 and up to 80, was set up in 1960.[[206]](#cite_note-207) and is a responsibility shared between [Mansfield District Council](/wiki/Mansfield_District "Mansfield District"), [Ashfield District Council](/wiki/Ashfield_District_Council "Ashfield District Council") and [Newark and Sherwood District Council](/wiki/Newark_and_Sherwood_District_Council "Newark and Sherwood District Council").[[207]](#cite_note-208)

There are other cemeteries on the [A60](/wiki/A60_road "A60 road") at [Mansfield Woodhouse](/wiki/Mansfield_Woodhouse "Mansfield Woodhouse") and at [Warsop](/wiki/Warsop "Warsop"), and off the [A617](/wiki/A617_road "A617 road") at [Pleasley Hill](/wiki/Pleasley_Hill "Pleasley Hill").[[200]](#cite_note-MDC_cemetery-201)

* [James Graham](/wiki/James_Graham_(playwright) "James Graham (playwright)") [OBE](/wiki/Order_of_the_British_Empire "Order of the British Empire") [FRSL](/wiki/Royal_Society_of_Literature "Royal Society of Literature") (born 1982), playwright and screenwriter. Known for the writings of the series of *[Sherwood](/wiki/Sherwood_(2022_TV_series) "Sherwood (2022 TV series)")* (2022), *[The Way](/wiki/The_Way_(TV_series) "The Way (TV series)")* (2024), *[Brian and Maggie](/wiki/Brian_and_Maggie "Brian and Maggie")* (2025) and *[Brexit: The Uncivil War](/wiki/Brexit:_The_Uncivil_War "Brexit: The Uncivil War")* (2019).
* [Richard Bacon](/wiki/Richard_Bacon_(broadcaster) "Richard Bacon (broadcaster)") (born 1976), broadcaster who was born and grew up in Mansfield.[[244]](#cite_note-245)[[245]](#cite_note-246) He presented *[Blue Peter](/wiki/Blue_Peter "Blue Peter")*, *[The Big Breakfast](/wiki/The_Big_Breakfast "The Big Breakfast")*, *[Good Morning Britain](/wiki/Good_Morning_Britain "Good Morning Britain")*, and on [Capital FM](/wiki/Capital_London "Capital London") and [BBC Radio 5 Live](/wiki/BBC_Radio_5_Live "BBC Radio 5 Live").
* [Tom Scott](/wiki/Tom_Scott_(presenter) "Tom Scott (presenter)") (born 1984 or 1985), presenter and former web developer.[[246]](#cite_note-:2-247)
* [Pollyanna Woodward](/wiki/Pollyanna_Woodward "Pollyanna Woodward") (born 1982), television presenter.[[247]](#cite_note-248)
* [Jim McGrath](/wiki/Jim_McGrath_(British_commentator) "Jim McGrath (British commentator)"), attended education in Mansfield.
* [Andrea Adams](/wiki/Andrea_Adams "Andrea Adams") (1946–1995), BBC broadcaster and journalist.
* [Calvin Robinson](/wiki/Calvin_Robinson "Calvin Robinson") (born 1985), writer and broadcaster.[[248]](#cite_note-Topping-2021-249)
* [Robert Dodsley](/wiki/Robert_Dodsley "Robert Dodsley") (1704–1767), playwright and poet, wrote *The King and The Miller of Mansfield* and *Sir John Cockle*. He suggested and financed the *[Dictionary of English Language](/wiki/A_Dictionary_of_the_English_Language "A Dictionary of the English Language")*.

### Miscellaneous

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mansfield&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Miscellaneous")]

* [Humphrey Ridley](/wiki/Humphrey_Ridley "Humphrey Ridley") (born 1653), physician and writer noted for studies into [neuroanatomy](/wiki/Neuroanatomy "Neuroanatomy"). He wrote the research paper of The Anatomy of the Brain, containing its Mechanism and Physiology. He wrote the first book written about the brain in the English language.[[249]](#cite_note-250)
* Sir [John Peace](/wiki/John_Peace "John Peace") (born 1949), co-founder of [Experian](/wiki/Experian "Experian"), former chairman of [Burberry](/wiki/Burberry "Burberry") and former [Lord Lieutenant of Nottinghamshire](/wiki/Lord_Lieutenant_of_Nottinghamshire "Lord Lieutenant of Nottinghamshire")[[250]](#cite_note-251)
* [John Radford (businessman)](/wiki/John_Radford_(businessman) "John Radford (businessman)") (born 1965) owner of [Mansfield Town F.C.](/wiki/Mansfield_Town_F.C. "Mansfield Town F.C.") and founder/owner of One Call Insurance.
* [Frank Cook](/wiki/Frank_Cook_(surgeon) "Frank Cook (surgeon)"), surgeon who had an interest in physiology. He was instrumental in founding the first department of gynaecological endocrinology.[[251]](#cite_note-252)
* [Agnes Catlow](/wiki/Agnes_Catlow "Agnes Catlow") (1806–1889), science writer who wrote a book on [conchology](/wiki/Conchology "Conchology").
* [Watson Fothergill](/wiki/Watson_Fothergill "Watson Fothergill") (1841–1928), Victorian architect
* [Elspeth Gibson](/wiki/Elspeth_Gibson "Elspeth Gibson") (born 1963), Nottingham-born fashion designer, studied at Mansfield College of Art and Design.[[252]](#cite_note-guardian-253) Her clients have included [Madonna](/wiki/Madonna "Madonna"), [Zara Phillips](/wiki/Zara_Phillips "Zara Phillips"), [Cate Blanchett](/wiki/Cate_Blanchett "Cate Blanchett"), and [Uma Thurman](/wiki/Uma_Thurman "Uma Thurman").
* [Samuel Jebb](/wiki/Samuel_Jebb "Samuel Jebb") (1694–1772), physician, nonjuror and literary scholar. Father of [Sir Richard Jebb, 1st Baronet](/wiki/Sir_Richard_Jebb,_1st_Baronet "Sir Richard Jebb, 1st Baronet") who was a royal physician to [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III").
* [Helen Wilson](/wiki/Helen_Mary_Wilson_(physician) "Helen Mary Wilson (physician)") (1864–1951), physician and social purity campaigner. Wilson became a surgeon at the [National Temperance Hospital](/wiki/National_Temperance_Hospital "National Temperance Hospital") in London. Her attitudes towards prostitution were progressive and she campaigned against its state regulation.[[253]](#cite_note-254)
* [Henry Pierrepont, 1st Marquess of Dorchester](/wiki/Henry_Pierrepont,_1st_Marquess_of_Dorchester "Henry Pierrepont, 1st Marquess of Dorchester") (born 1606), former English Peer[[254]](#cite_note-255)
